verses.text: 6:1–8:66 A construção do complexo do Templo e palácio e a celebração da dedicação do Templo são centrais para o relato de Salomão em 1 Reis. O Templo, erigido para glorificar a Deus, era o edifício mais importante de Israel, então o escritor de 1 Reis cuidadosamente registrou detalhes de sua construção. 6:1 O quarto ano de Salomão foi cerca de 967 a.C. Tratar o número 480 como representando anos do calendário rende uma data de cerca de 1446 a.C. para o êxodo de Israel do Egito. Aqueles que veem o número como simbólico (12 gerações vezes 40 anos cada) sugerem que o Êxodo ocorreu em data posterior, por volta de 1270 a.C. (cf. Introdução ao Livro de Êxodo, “A Data do Êxodo”).

6:2 Como o Tabernáculo antes dele, o Templo (literalmente casa) era o lugar onde Deus encontrava seu povo. Davi havia armazenado materiais para serem usados em sua construção (1Cr 22.2-4,14) e havia elaborado plantas para o Templo e as dado a Salomão (1Cr 28.11-12). A planta do Templo se assemelhava à do Tabernáculo, mas dobrava seu comprimento e largura e aumentava sua altura (cp. Êx 26.3,15-30; 36.26-34).
6:3-4 Diferentemente do Tabernáculo, o Templo tinha uma sala de entrada (ou pórtico) leste e duas colunas independentes diante da entrada (1Rs 7.15-22).
6:3-4 Diferentemente do Tabernáculo, o Templo tinha uma sala de entrada (ou pórtico) leste e duas colunas independentes diante da entrada (1Rs 7.15-22).

6:5 THE CHAMBERS THEREOF. (Kg1 6:5-10) against the wall of the house he built chambers—On three sides, there were chambers in three stories, each story wider than the one beneath it, as the walls were narrowed or made thinner as they ascended, by a rebate being made, on which the beams of the side floor rested, without penetrating the wall. These chambers were approached from the right-hand side, in the interior of the under story, by a winding staircase of stone, which led to the middle and upper stories.

6:6 And the nethermost chamber was five cubits broad,.... The nethermost row of them, which were upon the first floor: and the middle was six cubits broad, and the third was seven cubits broad; so that the middlemost was a cubit larger than the lowest, and the highest a cubit larger than that: the reason of which was, for without in the wall of the house he made narrowed rests round about; or rebatements; the thickness of the wall, as it was raised, became narrower at the height of every five cubits;

6:7 there was neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron heard in the house while it was in building—A subterranean quarry has been very recently discovered near Jerusalem, where the temple stones are supposed to have been hewn. There is unequivocal evidence in this quarry that the stones were dressed there; for there are blocks very similar in size, as well as of the same kind of stone, as those found in the ancient remains. Thence, probably, they would be moved on rollers down the Tyropean valley to the very side of the temple [PORTER, Tent and Kahn].

6:8 The door of the middle chamber was in the right side of the house,.... The south side of it: and they went up with winding stairs into the middle chamber; which were outside the chambers, and which winded about for the sake of taking up less room, and which led up to the door of the middle chamber, on the south of which they went into it; according to the Vulgate Latin and Tigurine versions, they went up in the forth of a cockle, or the shell of a snail;

6:9 Covered the house with beams and boards of cedar - The Eastern custom is very different from ours: we ceil with plaster, and make our floors of wood; they make their floors of plaster or painted tiles, and make their ceilings of wood. But it may not be improper to observe that, in ancient times, our buildings were somewhat similar. Westminster Hall is a proof of this.

6:10 chambers . . . five cubits high—The height of the whole three stories was therefore about fifteen cubits. they rested on the house with timber of cedar—that is, because the beams of the side stones rested on the ledges of the temple wall. The wing was attached to the house; it was connected with the temple, without, however, interfering injuriously with the sanctuary [KEIL].

6:11-13 O Senhor tinha uma mensagem encorajadora para Salomão. Como Deus apareceu a Salomão antes (1Rs 3.5) e depois (9.2) deste evento, esta mensagem provavelmente veio a ele por meio de um profeta. As palavras lembravam ao rei a promessa anterior de Deus (1Rs 3.14): se Salomão permanecesse fiel a Deus, o Templo que ele estava construindo se tornaria habitação eterna para Deus.

6:14-35 O foco muda para o interior do Templo. Embora o local do Templo não seja mencionado aqui, o Cronista o localizou na antiga eira de Araúna (1Cr 21.18-25; 2Cr 3.1). Este local é tradicionalmente pensado como a localização do monte Moriá (Gn 22.2). O Templo de Salomão ficava no canto nordeste de Jerusalém (agora chamada de “cidade velha”) na área agora associada com o Domo da Rocha.

6:15-18 revestido com madeira: As paredes internas eram revestidas de cedro do chão ao teto, de modo que nenhuma pedra externa pudesse ser vista. O efeito era requintadamente belo, refletindo cuidado meticuloso e grande despesa.

6:15-18 revestido com madeira: As paredes internas eram revestidas de cedro do chão ao teto, de modo que nenhuma pedra externa pudesse ser vista. O efeito era requintadamente belo, refletindo cuidado meticuloso e grande despesa.

6:19-22 Seguindo o padrão do Tabernáculo, o santuário interno, que era revestido . . . internamente com ouro sólido, era separado da sala principal do Templo por uma cortina (2Cr 3.14) e correntes de ouro. Como o Lugar Santíssimo no Tabernáculo, o santuário interno era um cubo perfeito. Abrigava a Arca da Aliança do Senhor que simbolizava a presença de Deus. O lugar da expiação ficava no topo da Arca (Êx 25.17); o sumo sacerdote anualmente fazia propiciação pelos israelitas aspergindo o sangue sacrificial da expiação sobre ela (Lv 16.1-19). • O altar que pertencia ao Lugar Santíssimo ficava na sala principal. Os sacerdotes queimavam incenso ali diariamente (Êx 30.34-38; 37.25-29). O altar era feito de cedro e coberto com ouro. • Como o santuário interno, a sala principal também era completamente revestida com ouro, o que lembrava aos sacerdotes que o Deus Todo-Poderoso estava presente. A sala principal também abrigava dez candelabros de ouro, dez mesas (2Cr 4.7-8) e outros mobiliários (1Rs 7.48-50).

6:23-28 No santuário interno havia dois querubins (cf. nota em 1Cr 28.18) feitos de madeira de oliveira e revestidos . . . com ouro. Estando como sentinelas voltados para a porta (2Cr 3.13), sua envergadura combinada alcançava de parede a parede. Junto com os dois querubins menores que se enfrentavam no topo da Arca (Êx 25.17-21), simbolizavam a presença assombrosa do Deus Santíssimo.

6:29 So also made he for the door of the temple,.... The holy place: posts of olive tree, a fourth part of the wall; which was five cubits, its breadth being twenty, Kg1 6:20; this door was a cubit wider than that into the most holy place, Kg1 6:31, more entering into the one than into the other; as more go into the church on earth than into the heavenly glory.

6:30 And the two doors were of fir tree,.... the posts were of olive, but the doors of fir, and they were both folding doors, as appears by what follows: the two leaves of the one door were folding, and the two leaves of the other door were folding; which, taking up less room, made the passage wider, see Ezek 41:24.

6:31 for the entering of the oracle—The door of the most holy place was made of solid olive tree and adorned with figures. The door of the holy place was made of cypress wood, the sides being of olive wood.

6:32 And he built the inner court,.... The court of the priests, 2 Chr 4:9; so called to distinguish it from the outer court, where the people assembled: this was built with three rows of hewed stone, and a row of cedar beams; the rows of stones were one upon another, topped with a row of cedar beams; or rather the cedar was a lining to the stones; and the whole is supposed to be about three cubits high, and was so low, that the people in the outward court might see priests ministering for them, and could converse with them;
6:33 In the fourth year was the foundation of the house of the Lord laid,.... The fourth year of Solomon's reign: in the month Zif; See Gill on Kg1 6:1.

6:34 In the eleventh year,.... That is, of the reign of Solomon: in the month Bul, which is the eighth month; from the month Abib or Nisan; this month answers to part of our October, and part of November; it is the same month sometimes called Marchesvan; it had the name of Bul, because of the rains which usually fell in this month, as Kimchi thinks, like a flood; and Noah's flood is called Mabbul, from the same root (i), and when all the fruits were withered, and the leaves were fallen from the trees;

6:35 He carved thereon cherubims and palm trees and open flowers: The doors to the inner sanctuary repeat the motifs of the walls (6:29) — angelic guardians, fruitful palms, and flowering openness. The same triad reappears in Ezekiel's visionary temple (Ezek 41:18) and the new Jerusalem's tree of life (Rev 22:2), figuring restored Eden.

6:36-38 O pátio interno cercando o Templo era restrito aos sacerdotes (2Cr 4.9) e continha o altar de bronze (2Cr 4.1), o Mar de bronze (1Rs 7.23-26; 2Cr 4.2-5) e dez carros que podiam segurar dez bacias de água para purificação (1Rs 7.27-40; 2Cr 4.6).

6:36-38 O pátio interno cercando o Templo era restrito aos sacerdotes (2Cr 4.9) e continha o altar de bronze (2Cr 4.1), o Mar de bronze (1Rs 7.23-26; 2Cr 4.2-5) e dez carros que podiam segurar dez bacias de água para purificação (1Rs 7.27-40; 2Cr 4.6).
6:38 meio do outono, no mês de Bul: O ano era 960 a.C.
